topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I stared at the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the mouse. "We've decided to move forward with other candidates," the subject line read. Other candidates. As if my years of grueling practice, the early mornings, the sacrifices I’d made—all of it was just statistical noise to them. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound I immediately clamped down on, forcing my jaw to lock. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring through the condensation on the glass. Every single success I’d chased felt like a cruel, elaborate joke played just for me. I felt a hot, prickly sensation rising up my neck, a tight knot of frustration coiling in my stomach that made breathing shallow. I needed to punch something, anything, to release this suffocating pressure.

[story 2]

"Unbelievable." The word was barely a whisper, but it vibrated with a sharp, brittle edge. I paced the length of the training facility, my boots clicking loudly on the polished floor. My chest felt tight, constricted, as if someone had physically squeezed the air out of my lungs. I stopped abruptly in front of the trophy case, my eyes sweeping over the gleaming hardware that represented everything I’d bled for. It felt obscene, a mockery. I turned, my voice rising in a strained tone, "They just don't *see* the effort. They don't appreciate the commitment." My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the tension radiating through my arms. I wanted to scream at the empty space, to demand an explanation, but the words caught, dissolving into a harsh, frustrated exhale. I spun around, kicking lightly at a discarded water bottle, the movement sharp and uncontrolled.

[story 3]

I reread the rejection letter three times, each time the ink seemed to burn my eyes. The silence in my room was deafening, amplifying the thumping rhythm of my own pulse. It was a heavy, insistent beat, demanding an outlet. I stood by my dresser, pulling at the fabric of my shirt, my movements jerky and agitated. A wave of intense heat washed over me, making my skin feel too tight, too sensitive. "This is ridiculous," I muttered, the sound rough in my throat. I walked over to the mirror, not to check my appearance, but to confront the raw fury churning behind my eyes. I stared at my reflection—a face etched with disappointment and simmering anger—and for a moment, I couldn't stop the tight, furious shake of my head. The injustice of it all felt like a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders, making it hard to draw a full breath.
